name: Hellasgården
highlights: The essential Stockholm wood-fired lake sauna, sitting directly on Källtorpssjön in Nacka Nature Reserve — 20 minutes from the city center. Nude bathing is the norm; mixed sessions on Mondays and Wednesdays. The sauna-lake-sauna cycle here is the closest Stockholm gets to the Finnish ideal.

name: Tanto Bastun
hours: Sat 09:00-10:45 (women), Sun 09:00-10:45 (mixed); public sessions via Billetto
highlights: A citizen-born non-profit sauna association founded by local initiative in 2015, sitting on a wooden pontoon on Årstaviken with panoramic views of Stockholm's skyline. Membership-driven but open to the public several times a week — everything about it feels like it belongs to the neighbourhood, because it does.

name: Sätrabastun
highlights: No booking, no fuss — just show up, pay 40 kronor by Swish and join if there's room. A sauna raft on Mälaren in working-class Skärholmen that opened in 2022, it embodies the Swedish value that sauna should be for everyone. About as unpretentious as it gets.

name: Ribersborgs Kallbadhus (Kallis)
hours: Summer (May-Aug): Mon-Tue & Thu-Fri 09:00-20:00, Wed 09:00-21:00, Sat-Sun 09:00-18:00; Winter (Sep-Apr): Mon-Tue & Thu-Fri 10:00-19:00, Wed 10:00-20:00, Sat-Sun 09:00-18:00
highlights: Malmö's beloved 1898 kallbadhus, nicknamed 'Kallis' and 'Ribban' by locals — a wooden pier jutting into the Öresund with five saunas, open-air pools for men and women, and monthly Queer Kallis nights. One of Sweden's finest surviving examples of the cold-bath house tradition, adored across generations.

name: Varbergs Kallbadhus
hours: Wed 13:00-20:00, Fri 11:00-17:00, Sat-Sun 09:00-17:00; daily in summer
highlights: A Moorish-style masterpiece built on wooden piles over the Kattegat in 1903 — onion towers, arched windows, and panoramic sea views from inside the sauna. Strictly nude, gender-separated, and completely timeless. One of the most architecturally remarkable bathhouses in all of Scandinavia.

name: Bastun i Jubileumsparken (Frihamnen)
highlights: Born from a citizens' co-design process for Gothenburg's 400th anniversary, this free public sauna made from recycled corrugated iron and 12,000 glass bottles won the regional architecture prize. Sits next to floating harbour pools on Göta älv — radical accessibility and community spirit baked into its DNA.

name: Saltholmens Kallbadhus
highlights: Over a century old and still fiercely nude, this bathhouse at the tip of the Gothenburg mainland has been a sanctuary for naked sea swimming since 1906. Three bathing areas — men, women, and mixed. A 30-minute tram ride from central Gothenburg, it's where the city's true cold-bath devotees go.

name: Hagabadet
highlights: Founded as the Renström Washhouse in 1876 with the motto 'For beauty, health and hygiene', Hagabadet is Gothenburg's grand historic bathhouse. Art Nouveau interior, thermal pools at 30-34°C and saunas at 55-80°C — it's the upscale end of the public bath tradition, firmly embedded in the fabric of the Haga neighbourhood.

name: Centralbadet
highlights: Built in 1904 in a gorgeous Art Nouveau courtyard building on Drottninggatan, Centralbadet is Stockholm's most historic urban spa. Five saunas, thermal pools, steam rooms and elegant tiled halls make this the closest thing Sweden has to a grand European Kurbad — visited by Stockholmers for over a century.

name: Kukkolaforsen — Rök-bastu (Sweden's Largest Smoke Sauna)
highlights: A complex of 13 saunas on the Torne River at the Swedish-Finnish border, anchored by Sweden's largest smoke sauna — a savusauna that holds 60 people. The smoke sauna tradition here is UNESCO-adjacent in its depth; the resort also runs the Swedish Sauna Academy. Cooling off in the rushing Torne River is non-negotiable.

name: Soukolojärvi Savusauna Village
highlights: Believed to contain the world's oldest smoke sauna, and the village with the highest concentration of smoke saunas anywhere in Sweden. A remote lakeside hamlet in the Torne Valley where the savusauna tradition has survived unbroken — this is not a visitor attraction, it's a living community practice.

name: Jukkasjärvi Culture Sauna
highlights: The cradle of the Swedish Sauna Academy, established by enthusiasts in one of Sweden's oldest villages on the Torne River. The 10-step Jukkasjärvi ritual — sauna, tar soap scrub, snow roll, river plunge, outdoor tub — is the most elaborated expression of northern Swedish sauna culture anywhere. 15 minutes from the Icehotel.

name: Solar Egg
address: Tuollavaara / TGA area, Kiruna (near new city centre)
highlights: A five-metre gold-plated egg-shaped sauna by artists Bigert & Bergström, commissioned for Kiruna as the town relocates away from the iron mine swallowing it. A wood-fired social sculpture open to locals and visitors — it travelled to Paris, Copenhagen, and Minnesota before returning home to Swedish Lapland. Fits eight people around a heart-shaped iron stove.

name: Arctic Bath
hours: Year-round (hotel guests and day spa bookings)
price: Spa day ~1 200 SEK; rooms from ~4 000 SEK/night
highlights: A ring-shaped floating spa on the Lule River that freezes into the ice each winter — guests step directly from wood-fired saunas into a hole cut in the river ice. Two Finnish saunas, a steam room, jacuzzis, and a cold bath fed by the living river. The most architecturally extraordinary sauna complex in Sweden.

name: Bastuflotten ReLaxa
hours: Season: May-October; bookable by the hour
price: 3 300 SEK first hour, 1 700 SEK/hour thereafter (up to 12 people)
highlights: A private-hire wood-fired sauna raft moored at Stocksund harbour, drifting through the northern Stockholm archipelago. The smell of birch smoke over open water, swim stops whenever you want, a gas grill on the sundeck — this is the quintessential Stockholm archipelago sauna experience.

name: Skärgårdens Kanotcenter — Bastu
price: ~1 200 SEK per group (up to 2 people, ~1.5 hours)
highlights: A traditional wood-fired sauna right at the kayak base in Vaxholm, 40 minutes from Stockholm — you tend the fire yourself and walk straight from the sauna into the Baltic Sea. Pre-warmed on arrival, combinable with kayak tours. The simplest, most accessible archipelago sauna experience that doesn't require chartering a boat.

name: Bastur.nu — Stora Essingen
highlights: Run by two sauna enthusiasts from Dalsland, this water-adjacent sauna on Stora Essingen island is marketed as 'Stockholm's most accessible sauna' — always by the water, always warm. A no-nonsense island sauna that keeps the focus entirely on heat and cold water.

name: STHLM Sauna Vinterviken
hours: Women, men, and mixed sessions (2 hours each); Wed open sessions for non-members 17:00-19:00
price: 500 SEK/year membership, 349 SEK/month; drop-in on Wednesdays
highlights: A member-managed community sauna in Vinterviken, south Stockholm — everyone who joins helps maintain the facility. Opened autumn 2022 with a strong neighbourhood ethos. The bay setting gives direct access to Mälaren for cold dips. A genuine grassroots bastun project rather than a commercial operation.

name: Navens Bastuflotte
address: Navudden, Kållandsö, near Lidköping, Lake Vänern
highlights: A 7×15-metre wood-fired sauna raft anchored on Europe's largest lake, with views across Dalbosjön and sunset over Vänern. Coffee made over an open fire is included; the grill hut stays warm all evening. A quintessentially Swedish lakeside evening — sauna, hot tub, swim in the lake, grill under an open sky.

name: Granö Beckasin — Älvbastu
address: Granö 14, 922 91 Granön, Västerbotten (1 hour NW of Umeå)
hours: Year-round; 105-minute private sessions for hotel guests
highlights: A classic Scandinavian wood-fired sauna sitting directly on the bank of the Umeälven river in deep Norrland forest. After the heat, you step down into the river — ice-cold in winter, bracing in summer. The surrounding tree-house lodge adds context: this is what wilderness sauna in northern Sweden genuinely looks like.

name: Umeå Kallbad & Bastu
hours: 06:00-23:00 general; Wed & Fri morning dips 07:00-08:00; Sun dips 10:00-12:00
highlights: Community-driven cold-bath and sauna association in the heart of Umeå, started with a converted wagon that became a symbol of neighbourhood solidarity. Located on Bölesholmarna, a small island within walking distance of the city centre. The ethos is classic Swedish: year-round bathing for everyone, no matter the season.

name: 7° Bad & Bastu — Marholmen
address: Marholmen, Roslagen archipelago, 1 hour north of Stockholm
price: Included with Mary's Havsbad package or bookable separately
highlights: Three converted bathhouses on a small island in the Roslagen archipelago, south-facing with a wooden jetty straight into the sea. Named after the 7°C average sea temperature — the point at which you feel most alive. No road access; the surrounding silence and birch forest are the main attraction.

name: Saxnäsgården Bastu — Lake Kultsjön
hours: Year-round (STF hostel guests and day visitors)
price: Included with accommodation or ~100-150 SEK day access
highlights: A classic STF (Swedish Tourist Association) mountain lodge on the shore of Lake Kultsjön in South Lapland, with Marsfjällen behind you and open water in front. The sauna sits at the lake edge — you step out into Kultsjön. Canoe hire, fishing, and Sami handicrafts complete an experience rooted in the old Swedish wilderness lodge tradition.

name: Buustamons Fjällgård — Utomhusbastu
hours: Year-round for lodge guests; day visitors by arrangement
highlights: The highest sauna on Åreskutan at 732 metres, with outdoor wood-fired hot tubs and a mountain stream for cooling. The lodge dates to the 18th century — 'Buusta' means wind shelter in old Jämtland dialect. In winter you roll in the snow; in summer you plunge in the stream. One of Åre's most authentically old-school mountain experiences.

name: Eden Bastun — Smedsudden
hours: Check eden.sthlm Instagram for current session times
highlights: A sauna anchored on Lake Mälaren at Smedsudden, housed in a turn-of-the-century villa next to Rålambshovsparken and the Västerbron bridge. The garden stretches over the little peninsula; the sauna floats beside it. Where the cultural side of Stockholm's sauna scene — wine, natural light, water views — comes together.

name: The Sacred Sauna
hours: Daily; check thesacredsauna.se for session times
price: ~350-450 SEK (guided 60-min ritual); lower for open sauna
highlights: Stockholm's dedicated aufguss and ritual sauna in the hip Slakthusområdet district — container-built complex with cold plunges, a patio, and guided sessions combining essential oils, breathwork, and music for up to 40 people. A self-service 'open sauna' option exists for purists who just want to sit in the heat.

name: Bastuflotte Gustavsberg
highlights: A wood-fired floating sauna in the central Stockholm archipelago at Gustavsberg — the historic porcelain town. You drift among skerries and pine-covered islands with a hot stove burning beside you. A more accessible alternative to chartering an entire boat, close enough to the city that you can make an afternoon of it.

name: Loka Brunn Spa
hours: Daily; check lokabrunn.se for current hours
highlights: Established as a spa town in 1720 and granted spa rights in 1759, Loka Brunn is one of Sweden's oldest health resorts — visited by Gustav III, Carl Linnaeus, and pilgrims before them. Set between two lakes in Bergslagen, the Water Salon on the lakeshore carries 300 years of wellness tradition into a still-functioning spa.

name: Bastuflotten Mariestad — Lake Vänern
address: Mariestad marina, Lake Vänern, Västra Götaland
highlights: A self-service floating sauna on Europe's largest lake — you book it, heat it yourself, and spend the afternoon drifting on Vänern. The complete Swedish freedom experience: no staff, no programme, just a wood-fired stove and 5 600 km² of freshwater around you. An ideal pit-stop on the Göta Canal route.

name: Big Branzino — Floating Sauna
address: Stockholm inner archipelago (mobile; based in Nacka/Värmdö area)
highlights: Designed by Sandellsandberg Arkitekter, Big Branzino is a steel catamaran with a timber-clad sauna at its heart and a distinctive bow-shaped roof. It navigates the Stockholm archipelago at five knots, stopping for swims between islands. Architecturally it is the most serious attempt to marry Stockholm's sauna and boat cultures into one vessel.

name: Ribersborgs Friluftsbad (outdoor section)
highlights: The free, open-air beach section of the Ribersborg stretch — a summer institution for Malmö residents. Not the historic kallbadhus building itself, but the long sandy beach and open-air bathing culture that surrounds it. Where Malmö locals head when they can't afford the 70 SEK entry fee or just want the wind in their face.

name: Dalälven Riverside Bastur — Dalarna
highlights: Dalarna's lake country is scattered with private and semi-public wood-fired saunas on the shores of Lake Siljan — the cultural heart of Sweden, where midsommar, folk music, and the sauna tradition intersect. The landscape of Maypoles and painted wooden houses through the sauna window is uniquely Swedish.

name: Relaxen Spa & Sauna
address: Kiruna city centre (check kirunalapland.se for current address post-relocation)
highlights: The everyday sauna for Kiruna residents — a standard fitness-centre-style sauna in the world's northernmost major city, where workers at LKAB's iron ore mine have bathed for generations. Not a tourist attraction but an honest look at how Lapland's working population uses the sauna.

name: Abisko Turiststation — Lakeside Bastu
hours: Year-round (STF lodge guests; some sessions open to day visitors)
highlights: A wood-fired sauna at the STF mountain station on the shore of Lake Torneträsk — one of Sweden's highest and most spectacular lakes in the shadow of Lapporten (the 'Gate of Lapland'). The Northern Lights are frequently visible from the sauna steps in winter. The most remote setting on this list.

name: Vätterstrands Campingbad & Bastu
highlights: A classic wood-fired lakeside sauna on the shore of Lake Vättern — Sweden's second-largest lake and famously cold and clear. After the heat, you walk into water so transparent you can see five metres down. Lake Vättern's purity makes the plunge here feel entirely different from murky urban alternatives.

name: Lomma Kallbadhus
highlights: A modest wood-fired kallbadhus on the Öresund coast between Malmö and Lund — a working-class seaside sauna beloved by locals from the surrounding flat Skåne farmland. The antithesis of the grand Varberg and Ribersborg kallbadhus: no Moorish domes, just planks, a stove, and cold sea.
